speaker: interviewer
text: This is January 28, 1996. The survivor's name is Sonia Hochman. Spelled H-O-C-H-M-A-N. Maiden name, Dodek, D-O-D-E-K. I am Marianne Kador, the interviewer. And the interview takes place in Great Neck, New York, in the USA. It's conducted in English. January 28, 1996. I'm the interviewer, Marianne Kador, K-A-D-O-R. I'm interviewing Mrs. Sonia Hochman Dodek at Great Neck, New York in her house. The interview is conducted in English. Good morning. Would you please tell us your name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sonia Hochman Dodek. Maiden name, Dodek. S-O-N-I-A H-O-C-H-M-A-N D-O-D-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on August 30, 1933 in the town of Korolowka, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is that spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-R-O-L-O-W-K-A. Korolowka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 30, 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Well, as a child-- take me back to the town of Korolowka and try to see through the eyes of a child. What kind of town was Korolowka?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a wonderful town. I was the first grandchild on both sides of the family. My father's name was Fischel Dodek. My mother's name was Henia Dodek. And I had grandparents on the Dodek side. There was a great-grandfather. His name was Mendel Dodek. My grandfather's name was Nichemia Dodek. My grandmother's name was Branchie Dodek. And there was seven children. My father was Fischel, then was Ruth, Velvel, Chanzel, Mirke, Eitke, and Charcie.

speaker: interviewer
text: All these people lived in Korolowka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And my mother's side, the Stermer family-- my grandmother's name was Esther Stermer. My grandfather's name was Zayde Stermer. And there was six children. My mother was the oldest. Her name was Henia. Then was Nisel, Chana, Shulim, Yetta, and Schloime. We were very comfortable in our town. My parents and grandparents were businesspeople. We had land. The land used to be rented out to peasants and the peasants would share the crop with us. And life was wonderful. I played a lot. My father was a businessman. He would go to Tarnopol, and to Stanislawow, to Lviv. And he would bring me home dolls and things. And I was a happy child. In our town, we were only 500 Jewish families. Our town was like this-- in the middle of the town there was a market, and the Jewish homes were surrounding the market. On the outskirts, the peasants lived. And after that you had a [NON-ENGLISH]-- a [NON-ENGLISH] was a big estate which belonged to Polish nobility with a lot of land. And the peasants would bring-- on Thursday, there was a marketplace in our town. The peasants would bring their produce and they would buy their goods from the Jewish people, from their stores-- because they had stores all around. And I remember my father would be always on the market, dealing and wheeling. He was an entrepreneur, a big businessmen in our town. And I would run after my father. They used to say, I run after my father like a [NON-ENGLISH], like a little dog. I had a lot of girlfriends. My best friend was Hanusha Holdenberg. Her grandmother had a big store, close to my grandmother Esther's house. And she also was an only child. We were both only children. This was a royalty, because in Europe there were a lot of children. And we were catered and hovered over. And we would play, and sing, and dance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in and out of each other's houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- yes. And I remember my father brought me home a pair of black patent leather shoes. And of course, in Korolowka, there was dust, and I used to run to my grandmother Esther on the other side of the street. My grandmother Esther had a big home. It was always a happy home. There were a lot of-- they were all young boys and girls. And they would sing-- and they would sing Hebrew songs because they had Zionist organizations in our town. And my mother took care of me and of my father. We had a house. We had a housekeeper. We had a woman to bring water, because there was no water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Running water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Running water in our town. But we lived wonderful for generations. Our people were brought out to Korolowka, to that vicinity for commerce. This is hundreds of years ago. Although in 1938, we started to hear that people were worried that Hitler might decide to invade Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a young child, from what did you pick up that worry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me tell you something. They used to call me an [NON-ENGLISH]-- since my aunts and uncles were all older than I was, I would run to their homes because they were-- one uncle, my uncle Sam, is like seven years older than me. Yetta is eight or nine years older than me. So I would run and listen what they were talking. They already understood what was going on, and I would pick up what they were talking. And my grandmother heard that there is a Canadian agency, a Jewish agency that people can go to Canada, if you have enough money to buy a farm. And if you have abled people, you can immigrate to Canada. They wanted to come to the United States, but because of the American quota they couldn't get in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my grandmother went to Chortkov and applied, and they accepted them. In fact, we were going to buy my grandmother's house. Chortkov was the nearest larger town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Bratslav and then Chortkov. Chortkov--

speaker: interviewer
text: How you spell Chortkov?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-O-R-T-O-V. I think. Or Chortkov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speaking Yiddish at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you speak Polish, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke Polish. I spoke Ukrainian. Because in our area, you had to speak both languages, Ukrainian and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go already to kindergarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. In 1939, I first started to go to kindergarten In 1938, my grandparents were going to sell their home and we were buying their house. Because in our town it was very hard to buy homes. The Jews lived all around.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if a person would die or would go away to a different country, that's the only time you could purchase a home. And you didn't want to leave the vicinity of where the Jewish families lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938, September the 10th, my sister Sima was born. And of course, we were very excited-- a new child came to the family. And she was so beautiful, like a little doll. And I was already five years old. So we were excited and life was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Though in 1939, I was playing outside with my children-- and we heard a lot of noise. We used to play in the street, and make like boxes and jump from one box to the other. Throw a penny, or a piece of glass, and jump, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, we heard a lot of noise. And we looked up, and we saw an army was coming to our town. That was when Hitler and Stalin made a pact. When Poland was divided. Our side went to Russia. Well, when the Russians came in, I was a young little girl. And it was exciting-- were armies, there were soldiers, you know. And that's when I started to go to kindergarten. I started to go to kindergarten in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish kindergarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian. The Russians were there. Right. Well, when the Russians came in, they started to liquidate our land. Of course, businessmen were not allowed to do business. Since my father was a big businessman, one day, they came and they looked for him to grab him to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father and mother started to run away. First they hid in Borschtov by [NON-ENGLISH], in his house. And then they were hiding in Stanislaw in a town in Poland, Kolomyia, whoever would let them in.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and I were left between the two grandparents' homes. And one day-- my grandmother Esther was a very smart lady. She had a big home. A house-- it was a big house and she had a big gate all around the house. And she had barns and cellars for wheat and for hay for the horses. It was a [GERMAN] they call it a [GERMAN] in German. It's like a small estate.

speaker: interviewer
text: My grandmother said, you know what? Why don't we make a [NON-ENGLISH]? A [NON-ENGLISH] is a bunker-- and let Fischel hide in my house. Because my mother would come home, and take a look on my sister and I are doing, and then she would leave for another few weeks. The children are very lonely. And at least, the children will have their mother. I didn't know where my father was hiding because they were afraid to tell children. In case children-- the KGB used to come around and ask, where is he? So this is exactly what happened. They made a bunker for my father. My father was hiding in the bunker.

speaker: interviewee
text: And like, this was going on until about 1941. In summer of 1941, we started to hear that it might become that the Russians might go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you already pick up that attitude of your friends' change towards you, or your life wasn't the same as it was before? Can you remember that, as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainians started to feel that the Germans are going to come into our town. And they started to show. They would rob people, they would beat up people, they would throw stones through the windows in the villages. In our town nothing happened yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you heard about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we heard about it. Yes, yes. Well, in December of '41, we started to see people running through our town. Our town was like on the road from Romania. You crossed through our town. And a lot of people were running. And they said that the Russians are going back. They're pulling back to their border. And they were following the Russian army. Unfortunately, what happened is by the time they got to Kiev, the Germans were there already. In our town, the Hungarians came in for a week or two. They came in and they put up their flag. And they weren't violent or anything. But in the surrounding, all around us, the Ukrainians killed an awful lot of people-- especially near Romania. Some of the people were running. They used to find bodies chopped up in the-- near the river. One woman came from Romania. Her whole family was killed. Her name was Mrs. Edelman. My mother took her in-- she felt sorry for her, and she lived with us. Well, two weeks or three weeks, the Hungarians were-- and then the Germans came in. And this is really when my story starts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you start the story, you mentioned that you spoke at home Yiddish. Was this an orthodox family you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody was orthodox in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my town, most of the people were orthodox. I remember I used to go to shul with my father. My father would come home Friday after business. Take out the money from his pockets, put the money away, and prepare for Shabbos. On Shabbos, we would go to the synagogue. My father used to love to pray at the pulpit. He davened like a Chassan. He had such a beautiful voice. And I used to go carry the tallis for my father to shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there more than one synagogues in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In our town, there was synagogues. My parents and my grandparents belonged to the businessmen's synagogues-- the synagogue. And they had the people, workers, your know, the Arbeiters. They were like the craftsman, the shoemakers, the furriers, the dressmakers, or the tailors. They had also a synagogue. And there were another one or two synagogues, that I don't remember exactly. But we know we belonged to a very big synagogue. It was in the middle of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Germans came in, what happened to not just individuals but Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened is they called the elders together from the town. And they started the Judenrat. And they asked for young boys to into the-- they made a Jewish police. And their first decree, of course, was that all of us should wear Jewish stars. Even the children we all had to wear a Jewish star. Well, you wear it here on the lapel, or you wear it on the arm. But we all wore Jewish stars. And they-- the-- Jews were confiscated. Land was confiscated immediately. We're not allowed to have no horses, no cows, no animals, nothing. And they started to give us decrees. In fact, they came to my grandparents that one of the boys should be in the police. My grandmother said, no. She didn't want any of her sons to police other Jewish people. Well, the Judenrat started to give us decrees. And the first decree was they wanted fur. A Jew was not allowed to have any fur. All the furs. Whatever you had-- a fur jacket, a fur coat. My father used to have those big fur coats, the [NON-ENGLISH] they used to call it. You know, when he used to go on business in the winter, because it was very cold. Everything had to be delivered to the Judenrat. And they would come and they would confiscate everything with trucks. In fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was this explained to you as a five, six year old child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you-- I knew about the Judenrat. My best girlfriend, Hanusha, her father was in the Judenrat. So she used to run to see her father and I would run with her. So I saw what was going on in the Judenrat. And we use to run, because the Judenrat was like in the middle of the town. They took a big building and they made a Judenrat out of it. And she would go to see her father and we were always together. So I would run into the Judenrat and I would see all those aktions. And we would listen, and we understood. We were already at that time, eight and a half years old. And children of eight and a half during the war were grownup children-- we understood everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are now in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're in '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're in like in September of '41. Then one day the Germans came and they said to the Judenrat, we need young men to go to work. We're building roads, and we need men from your town. And they asked for a few hundred boys. The Judenrat came and told the parents, if you give us your boys, in a few months another 50 or 60 boys will come. We'll keep-- they will rotate them. And one of-- they went away. They came with trucks, they took them away to those slave labor camps. Somebody took off the Jewish armband. A woman put on a shawl, and went like an Aryan-- papers-- to see where these boys were. Well, when she came and she saw, there were three camps that I know of. There was Borke-- it was in Borke Wielki, there was a slave labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Borke?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-O-R-K-E. There was Kamnionka.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is that spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-M-N-I-O-N-K-A. Kamnionka. And Stupka. S-T-U-P-K-A. And she came back and she told us what was happening with these boys. If one of them tried to run away, 10 of them were shot. They were beaten, they were starved, they were digging trenches, and they were making roads. Those camps were not too far away from Lviv. After Tarnopol, closer to-- between Tarnopol and Lviv. And then they used to come, the Germans, practically every week. And they're running. When you saw the Germans come-- grabbing men for slave labor camp, because the boys knew if they were caught, they were destined for death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember that you were scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scared, frightened. You used to shiver. I'm still shaking when I think about it. Because when you saw Germans, you knew that a great catastrophe is going to happen any minute. You didn't know what was going to happen. Well, one day it was Passover time. This was already in 1942, Passover time. The Germans made a trap for all the young men in our area. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What happened was, they said that all the boys over 13, up to the age of 50, have to come to Borschtov, and there they will have-- they have tables set up, and they will give them passports. If they will be caught without those passports, they are going to be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people started to look to-- to hire a peasant. Because we had-- we had a ghetto. But our ghetto was not surrounded by barbed wire, since we all-- the Jews lived all in-- in-- surrounded in a circle like. So the Ukrainian police were watching us. When did people-- when were people forced to go or move to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away, as soon as the Germans came in, the surrounding villages, all the people came to our ghetto and they made a ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- no, '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: '41. Because we-- '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: People moved into your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandmother's sister, Leicha Wechsler, and her son Shermer Wechsler, and her son Luncher Wechsler came to my house. And my grandmother had a very big house. So her other sister, Chencha Kimmelman and Mendel Kimmelman-- and their daughter, [NON-ENGLISH], Charna, and Schloime, their son, moved into them. And most of the people lived with other people. You know, the people shared homes. Well, going back to the-- to the trap that they made for our men. All the men started to go. My grandmother said to her sons, wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father got dressed, he was going. My grandma says, where are you going? Just take it easy. Don't run.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I'm to Borschtov. Because he had an uncle. He says, I'll go and I'll see my aunt and uncle. She says, just a minute. You wait. And she took off her Jewish star, she put on a shawl, and she started to walk up the road. She said she was going to go-- it was a market day that day. And she was going to go and try to see. Make believe she's a peasant and see what was going on. And she didn't have to leave our town. Because another girl did the same thing, because she came running. She said, the people are being let in, and nobody's-- they don't let nobody out. That trap was made for five towns-- Borschtov, Korolowka, Mielec, Szcala, and I think Clustov. Anyway, all these men were trapped and they were never heard of. And we started to see that we can't trust the Germans, because what they tell us is never true. And they're just trapping us. They're just tricking us. Because we're Jews, we were hoping-- we were trying to believe them. We were hoping maybe, maybe they'll take some boys, and they'll leave the population alone. But then, all of a sudden, we started to hear that there's such a thing as-- before I go to the aktias, I'd like to tell you. A man from Borschtov, from the Judenrat, came to our town. And he really came-- he knew my father was a businessman. He knew he had money, to my grandparents. And he said, I have permission from the Gestapo from Borschtov to-- to take a few men, and give them badges. They would have a badge. And with this badge, they would have a permit-- if they have enough money to pay off the Gestapo. And then you have to pay the Gestapo like every month-- and they have enough money to buy a horse and wagon back from the peasants, they can go in the villages and collect scrap iron and old materials for the German government. Three people from our family bought those badges. My father, Fischel Dodek bought that badge, my grandfather Zayde Stermer bought that badge, and my uncle Nisel Stermer. And my God, this was a miracle. They could leave our town, and they could bring us back a bread, a piece of bread, a chicken-- I don't think we never had a chicken. You know, but bread-- they could bring back a little bit of flour, some potatoes, you know. And they were free to travel around in the villages. And that's when they started-- the peasants and some people were telling them that they're having aktions in other towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they describe what aktion really meant at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wanted to know what is an aktia? What is an action? They say they come in, they surround the town, the ghetto, and they come in, and they take everybody out. People started to scramble to look for-- to make bunkers, to hide. In my grandmother Esther's house there were six bunkers. Not one bunker was ever discovered.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: And under the ground, yeah. What can I tell you? Each one of our family was trying to make bunkers for themselves-- that scrambling of bunkers. I remember as a child, because the digging had to be done at night. Because you had neighbors-- Ukrainians and Poles were surrounding us. And they could hear the knocking and the banging. My grandmother's house, they had six bunkers. And my house was a smaller house. And we only had one bunker, and it didn't look too safe. So my sister, Sima, and myself, and my mother we moved in, and my father, we moved into my grandparents' house. And my grandmother started to have rehearsals-- how long it would take us to get into that bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the bunker look inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were six bunkers. The bunker that my grandmother-- in order to be with her daughter and small children-- don't forget, my sister is three-- three and a half years old, and I'm eight and a half years old. She decided she's going to be with her daughter and the children in the bunker. Because nobody wanted to be with children in the bunkers. Because children would cry, they would make noise, they had to make, they were hungry. So my grandmother decided that she would be with my mother, and myself, and my sister Sima in the bunker. Our bunker was in a stall, you know where horses stand. And then there was like a big thing where the horses feed. Underneath that, they dug out enough room for all of us to go in. And there was a cover. The cover was camouflaged with straw. You would pick it up. And then the cover you would pull the cover down. The straw would fall down all around you. And this is where we were. One day, I was near the Judenrat playing with my girlfriend, Hanusha. And we see a German comes out from the Judenrat. I was not aware that there was a German inside because I would not have gone with the Judenrat with her, because she wanted to see her father. And the German comes out with the head of the Judenrat, with Max Glickstern. And my-- and we're walking in, and he says, ah, the kinder, they look good-- they look good. Those children look good. He says, those are children from our Judenrat. He says. OK. And he walked away. That day was the Jewish holidays. He came to our town-- the head of the Gestapo. I didn't know it was the head of the Gestapo. Later on, I heard the grownups talk that that was him. And he asked for money, and jewelery, and American dollars. And he said, if we do that, he won't make an aktion in our town. So, of course, right away everybody, the people that had money, the Judenrat called together. And he said, listen, we have to save the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whatever anyone had, they gave. And we were hoping they will be spared. That they will leave us alone, at least for a while-- that we'll live.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Rosh Hashona passed by. No aktion. That Rosh Hashona, all of us were already in our home. Nobody went to the synagogue. Everybody was afraid to go, to leave the homes. We all were in our homes. Yom Kippor passed by, and no aktion. So we were excited. The German kept his word. Right after Yom Kippor, my father-- since he had permission to travel around-- the people in our town and some of his friends were called to the slave labor camp. The parents were begging him if he could take some packages to them, because the boys were starving. So he said yes. Evidently, he got permission from the Judenrat to take packages. He was such a gallant man. Such a brave person. He wasn't afraid of anything. Anyway, he went away right after Yom Kippor. He must have been delayed to deliver those packages, because first day of Sukkot, early in the morning, we were sleeping by my grandmother in the house. My grandmother would stand watch like a watchman. She wouldn't sleep. What happened was she would took out a small pane from one of the windows, and she would listen what was going on on the street. If you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's continue in a second, please.
